items.q: What does 'Rethink' mean in the 6 Rs?
items.correct: Questioning whether a product is necessary or could be designed differently for sustainability.
items.misconception: Trying a design again if it does not work first time.
items.why: Students apply 'rethink' to design iteration. In sustainability, it means challenging the NEED for a product altogether, or fundamentally rethinking how it's made - a more radical step than reducing materials.

items.q: Why can thermoplastics be recycled but thermosets cannot?
items.correct: Thermoplastics soften when heated and can be remoulded; thermosets have cross-links that lock the shape permanently.
items.misconception: Thermosets are too dangerous to recycle.
items.random_wrong: Thermoplastics are cheaper, so it is worth recycling them.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Thermosets undergo a one-way curing reaction creating cross-links; reheating them just degrades them without remelting. Thermoplastics have no cross-links so they remelt freely.

items.q: If voltage is doubled and resistance is unchanged, what happens to the current?
items.correct: Current doubles (V = IR, so I = V/R).
items.why: Students get the direction of proportionality wrong. Current is PROPORTIONAL to voltage (when R is constant) - more push, more flow. They sometimes confuse this with current being inversely proportional to resistance.

items.q: Whose 2005 Double Diamond models the design process?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: UK Design Council. “IDEO” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which technique systematically alters product attributes?
items.why: Correct answer: SCAMPER. “TRIZ” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many diamonds are in the Double Diamond model?
items.why: The real figure is 2 diamonds. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
